meta.title: Cloud Computing
meta.subtitle: build & operate on the cloud
meta.blurb: From cloud fundamentals and provider ecosystems to containers, serverless, IaC, security, and observability — the modern cloud engineering stack.

t-cloud-overview.label: Cloud Computing Overview
t-cloud-overview.desc: What cloud computing is, why it matters, and the core value propositions — on-demand resources, elasticity, and pay-per-use.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud computing delivers **compute, storage, and networking** over the internet on demand. You rent what you need, scale it up or down, and pay only for what you use.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Five essential characteristics (NIST): **on-demand self-service**, broad network access, **resource pooling**, rapid elasticity, and measured service. The **shared responsibility model** splits security duties between provider and customer.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: By 2026 the public cloud market exceeds **$800 billion**. Most enterprises run multi-cloud (~75%), picking each provider for its strengths rather than going all-in. The real skill isn't clicking consoles — it's designing systems that exploit cloud primitives cost-effectively.

t-service-models.label: Service Models (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS)
t-service-models.desc: The three layers of cloud abstraction — how much you manage vs. how much the provider manages.

links.title: Google — IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **IaaS** gives you virtual machines and networks (you manage the OS up). **PaaS** gives you a platform to deploy code (provider manages runtime). **SaaS** gives you finished software (you're just a user).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **FaaS** (Functions-as-a-Service) sits between PaaS and SaaS — you deploy individual functions and pay per invocation. **CaaS** (Containers-as-a-Service) is another hybrid: managed container orchestration.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Most production systems mix layers: IaaS for custom workloads, PaaS for managed databases, SaaS for collaboration. The trend is toward higher abstraction — less ops, more product work — but you trade control for convenience.

t-providers.label: Cloud Providers
t-providers.desc: The big three — AWS, Azure, GCP — their strengths, market share, and when to pick which.

links.title: KodeKloud — AWS vs Azure vs GCP 2026

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **AWS** (largest, broadest catalog — 30% of global cloud infrastructure spend in Q1 2026), **Azure** (deepest Microsoft/enterprise integration — 25%), and **GCP** (strongest in data analytics and AI/ML — 13%) together control ~68% of global cloud spend. Google Cloud grew revenue 63% YoY — the fastest of any hyperscaler — driven by AI infrastructure.

levels.label: Strengths
levels.body: AWS: mature ecosystem, most services (~200+), huge community. Azure: hybrid cloud (Arc), Office 365/AD integration, government compliance. GCP: BigQuery, Kubernetes (invented it), TPUs, Gemini AI.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: AI/ML is the biggest differentiator in 2026 — Azure for OpenAI/GPT models, GCP for TPUs and BigQuery ML, AWS for the broadest GPU selection and SageMaker. Most enterprises go multi-cloud, picking each provider for genuine strength rather than all-in on one.

t-well-architected.label: Well-Architected Frameworks
t-well-architected.desc: Design principles for reliable, secure, efficient, cost-effective, and sustainable cloud systems.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Each major provider publishes a **Well-Architected Framework** — a set of design principles and best practices for building production-quality cloud systems.

levels.label: The pillars
levels.body: AWS defines six pillars: **Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability**. Azure and GCP have equivalent frameworks with similar themes.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Run a Well-Architected Review before and after major launches. The frameworks are checklists — they won't design your system, but they'll catch common gaps in security, resilience, and cost that teams miss under delivery pressure.

t-vms.label: Virtual Machines
t-vms.desc: The foundational IaaS primitive — on-demand servers you configure and manage.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **virtual machine** is a server you rent by the hour (or second). Pick an instance type (CPU, RAM, GPU), attach storage, assign a network — you control the OS.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Instance families optimise for different workloads: **general purpose** (balanced), **compute-optimised**, **memory-optimised**, **GPU/accelerator**. Use **auto-scaling groups** to match capacity to demand automatically.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use **spot/preemptible** instances for fault-tolerant batch work (60–90% savings). Right-size aggressively — most VMs are overprovisioned. For stateless workloads, prefer containers or serverless; keep VMs for legacy apps or workloads needing full OS control.

t-containers.label: Containers & Docker
t-containers.desc: Lightweight, portable runtime packages — the unit of deployment for modern cloud-native apps.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **container** packages your app and all its dependencies into a single image that runs the same everywhere — laptop, CI, production.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Built on Linux namespaces and cgroups. **Docker** popularised the format; the OCI standard defines images and runtimes. Images are layered and stored in **registries** (ECR, ACR, Artifact Registry, Docker Hub).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Containers are the default deployment unit for microservices. Keep images minimal (distroless/alpine), scan for CVEs in CI, and run as non-root. For orchestration at scale, you need Kubernetes or a managed container service (ECS, Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps).

t-kubernetes.label: Kubernetes
t-kubernetes.desc: The de facto standard for container orchestration — automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerised applications.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Kubernetes (K8s)** automates deploying, scaling, and healing containers across a cluster. You declare desired state; K8s makes it real.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Core objects: **Pods** (smallest unit), **Deployments** (rollouts), **Services** (stable networking), **Ingress** (external access). **Helm** charts package complex apps. A **service mesh** (Istio, Linkerd) adds mTLS, traffic control, and observability.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026 Kubernetes v1.36 is the universal control plane — managing containers, serverless (Knative), ML workloads, and even databases. In-place pod resource updates (CPU/memory changes without restart) reached GA in v1.35, and image volumes allow pulling OCI images as read-only volumes. Use managed K8s (EKS, GKE, AKS) to avoid control-plane ops.

t-serverless.label: Serverless & FaaS
t-serverless.desc: Run code without managing servers — event-driven, auto-scaling, pay-per-invocation.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Serverless** means you deploy code (functions) and the provider handles all infrastructure — provisioning, scaling, patching. You pay per execution, not per hour.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **FaaS** (Lambda, Azure Functions, Cloud Functions) executes event-driven functions. Triggers include HTTP, queues, storage events, and schedules. **Cold starts** add latency on first invocation — mitigate with provisioned concurrency or lightweight runtimes.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Serverless is ideal for event-driven, sporadic, and unpredictable workloads. Containers win for high-volume, long-running, stateful apps. In 2026 the line blurs — serverless containers (Fargate, Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps) give you both: no infra management with full runtime control.

t-networking.label: Cloud Networking
t-networking.desc: Virtual networks, subnets, load balancers, DNS, and connectivity — the plumbing that connects everything.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **VPC** (Virtual Private Cloud) is your isolated network in the cloud. You define subnets, route tables, and firewalls to control traffic flow.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Public subnets** face the internet (via IGW); **private subnets** are internal-only (use NAT for outbound). **Load balancers** (ALB/NLB, Azure LB, Cloud LB) distribute traffic. **DNS** (Route 53, Cloud DNS) routes users; **CDNs** (CloudFront, Akamai) cache at the edge.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Design for least-access: default-deny security groups, private subnets for data tiers, VPC peering or Transit Gateway for multi-account. In 2026 the network is software-defined — treat network config as code and version it alongside your infrastructure.

t-iam.label: Identity & Access Management
t-iam.desc: Who can do what — authentication, authorization, roles, policies, and least privilege.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **IAM** answers: who are you (authentication) and what can you do (authorization)? Every cloud action is governed by policies attached to identities.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Policies** define allowed/denied actions on resources. Assign them to **roles**, not individual users. Use **federation** (SAML, OIDC) to connect your identity provider. Always enforce **MFA** for humans.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026 IAM is the control layer across every identity type. Enforce **least privilege** rigorously — use tools like IAM Access Analyzer (AWS) or Recommender (GCP) to find over-permissioned roles. Prefer short-lived credentials and **JIT (just-in-time) access** over standing admin privileges.

t-zero-trust.label: Zero Trust Architecture
t-zero-trust.desc: Never trust, always verify — a security model where network location alone grants no access.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Zero Trust** means: trust nothing implicitly, verify everything explicitly, and minimize blast radius by assuming breach. Network reachability is not permission.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Every request is checked against **identity, device, location, and policy** before access is granted. **Microsegmentation** limits lateral movement. **SASE/ZTNA** replaces traditional VPN for remote access.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Google pioneered this with **BeyondCorp**. In 2026, zero trust is mainstream: AWS Verified Access, Azure Conditional Access, GCP BeyondCorp Enterprise. JIT privilege elevation removes standing admin access — engineers request elevation for a specific purpose and duration.

t-cloud-security.label: Cloud Security Posture
t-cloud-security.desc: Encryption, compliance, CSPM, and the security hygiene that keeps your cloud environment hardened.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud security = **encrypt** data at rest and in transit, **log** everything, **restrict** access to least privilege, and **scan** for misconfigurations.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Use **CSPM** tools (AWS Security Hub, Defender for Cloud, SCC) to detect drift from baselines (CIS Benchmarks). Enable **tamper-resistant logging** (CloudTrail, Audit Logs). Block public storage by default.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Most cloud breaches are misconfigurations, not zero-days. Automate guardrails: SCPs (AWS), Azure Policy, Org Policies (GCP) prevent dangerous actions before they happen. Shift security left — scan IaC in CI, not after deployment.

t-storage.label: Cloud Storage
t-storage.desc: Object, block, and file storage — choosing the right tier for durability, performance, and cost.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Three types: **Object storage** (S3, Blob, GCS) for unstructured data at any scale. **Block storage** (EBS, Managed Disks) for VM disks. **File storage** (EFS, Azure Files) for shared file systems.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Object storage offers **11 nines of durability** and tiered pricing: hot (frequent access), cool, cold/archive. Lifecycle policies auto-transition objects to cheaper tiers as they age.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Object storage is the default for data lakes, backups, static assets, and ML datasets. Enable versioning and replication for critical data. Block storage matters for databases and latency-sensitive workloads — choose between SSD (gp3/io2) and HDD based on IOPS needs.

t-databases.label: Managed Databases
t-databases.desc: Relational, NoSQL, and purpose-built databases as managed services — less ops, more focus on data.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Managed databases handle backups, patching, replication, and failover for you. Pick **relational** (PostgreSQL, MySQL) for structured data with joins; **NoSQL** (DynamoDB, Cosmos DB, Firestore) for flexible schemas at scale.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Purpose-built** databases: time-series (Timestream), graph (Neptune), ledger (QLDB), wide-column (Bigtable), in-memory cache (ElastiCache/Redis). Choose by access pattern, not brand.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Aurora and AlloyDB offer PostgreSQL-compatible engines with cloud-native storage for high throughput. **Spanner** provides global strong consistency. In 2026, serverless database tiers (Aurora Serverless v2, Cosmos serverless, Neon) scale to zero for dev/test and scale up for production — pay only for what you use.

t-messaging.label: Messaging & Event Streaming
t-messaging.desc: Decoupling services with queues, pub/sub, and event streams — the backbone of async architectures.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Queues** (SQS, Azure Queue) decouple producers from consumers. **Pub/Sub** (SNS, Google Pub/Sub) fans out events to multiple subscribers. **Event streams** (Kafka, Kinesis) provide ordered, replayable event logs.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Queues guarantee at-least-once delivery and handle backpressure. Pub/Sub adds topic-based routing. Event streaming (Kafka, MSK, Confluent Cloud) enables real-time analytics, CDC, and event sourcing with durable, partitioned logs.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: **EventBridge** (AWS) and **Eventarc** (GCP) are the modern event routers — schema-aware, filtered, with native service integrations. For high-throughput streaming, managed Kafka (MSK, Confluent) beats self-hosted. Design for idempotent consumers — duplicates happen.

t-iac.label: Infrastructure as Code
t-iac.desc: Define, version, and automate cloud infrastructure using code — Terraform, Pulumi, OpenTofu, and CDKs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **IaC** means defining cloud resources in code files that live in version control, are reviewed like app code, and deployed through automated pipelines. No more clicking consoles.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Terraform** (HCL, declarative, 4800+ providers) is the most mature. **Pulumi** uses real languages (TypeScript, Python, Go). **OpenTofu** is the open-source Terraform fork. **CDKs** (AWS CDK, CDKTF) generate IaC from imperative code. All use a **plan → apply** loop with state tracking.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, IaC is a three-way choice: **Terraform 1.15** (mature ecosystem, 4800+ providers), **Pulumi 5.0** (full programming languages — TypeScript, Python, Go), **OpenTofu 1.12** (CNCF Sandbox since April 2025, drop-in Terraform replacement with state encryption). Use **remote state** and **locking**, modularise with reusable modules, and run `plan` in CI so reviewers see exactly what will change. Drift detection and policy-as-code (OPA, Sentinel) catch misconfigurations before apply.

t-cicd.label: CI/CD Pipelines
t-cicd.desc: Continuous Integration and Delivery — automating build, test, and deployment for fast, reliable releases.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **CI** automatically builds and tests every commit. **CD** automatically deploys passing builds to staging or production. Together they shorten the feedback loop from days to minutes.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Pipelines: lint → build → unit test → integration test → security scan → deploy. Use **environments** (dev/staging/prod) with promotion gates. **GitOps** (Argo CD, Flux) uses Git as the source of truth — a merged PR *is* a deployment.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, GitOps is the dominant pattern for Kubernetes deployments. Combine with IaC so infra and app deploy through the same pipeline. Progressive delivery (canary, blue-green, feature flags) reduces blast radius. Keep pipelines fast (<10 min) — slow CI kills developer velocity.

t-observability.label: Observability & Monitoring
t-observability.desc: Understanding system behaviour through metrics, logs, and traces — powered by OpenTelemetry.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Observability = understanding **what's happening inside** your system from its external outputs. The three pillars: **metrics** (numbers over time), **logs** (events), and **traces** (request journeys).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **OpenTelemetry (OTel)** is the dominant open-source standard — vendor-neutral APIs for instrumentation, an OTel Collector for routing, and exporters to any backend (Prometheus, Jaeger, Datadog, CloudWatch). In 2026, **continuous profiling** emerges as the fourth signal.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Instrument with OTel SDK, collect with OTel Collector, store in Tempo/Jaeger (traces), Prometheus/Mimir (metrics), Loki (logs). Trace context propagates automatically via **W3C headers**. Start with auto-instrumentation, add manual spans for business logic. The goal is meaningful, cost-efficient observability — not just collecting more data.

t-cost-ops.label: FinOps & Cost Optimisation
t-cost-ops.desc: Managing and optimising cloud spend — visibility, accountability, and right-sizing.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud bills grow fast without governance. **FinOps** is the practice of bringing financial accountability to cloud — teams own their spend.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Three phases: **Inform** (tagging, dashboards, cost allocation), **Optimise** (right-sizing, spot instances, reserved capacity, storage tiering), **Operate** (budgets, alerts, anomaly detection).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Cloud costs climb ~22% year-over-year without active management. Top levers: terminate idle resources, right-size VMs (most are overprovisioned), use spot/preemptible for fault-tolerant work, commit to Savings Plans for steady-state. Automate with cost anomaly alerts and scheduled scale-down in non-prod.

t-microservices.label: Microservices & API Gateways
t-microservices.desc: Decomposing monoliths into small, independently deployable services connected through APIs.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Microservices** split a system into small services, each owning one business capability, deployable independently. They communicate over APIs (REST, gRPC, events).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: An **API Gateway** (AWS API Gateway, Apigee, Kong) sits in front — handling auth, rate limiting, routing, and versioning. Decompose by **domain** (DDD bounded contexts), not by tech layer.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Microservices trade code complexity for operational complexity. You need CI/CD per service, distributed tracing, contract testing, and clear ownership. Don't split prematurely — start monolithic, extract services when team/scale boundaries demand it.

t-dr-ha.label: Disaster Recovery & High Availability
t-dr-ha.desc: Designing systems that survive failures — multi-AZ, multi-region, RPO/RTO, and chaos engineering.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **High Availability** = staying up during routine failures. **Disaster Recovery** = getting back up after catastrophic failures. Both are about limiting downtime and data loss.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data you can afford to lose. **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = how fast you must recover. Strategies range from backup/restore (cheap, slow) to **multi-region active-active** (expensive, instant).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Deploy across at least **2 AZs** for HA. For DR, use pilot-light or warm-standby in a second region. Test recovery regularly — **chaos engineering** (AWS FIS, Gremlin, Litmus) injects real failures to prove resilience before an outage hits you in production.

t-hybrid-cloud.label: Hybrid & Multi-Cloud
t-hybrid-cloud.desc: Spanning on-premises and multiple clouds — Arc, Outposts, Anthos, and multi-cloud strategy.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Hybrid cloud** connects on-premises data centres with public cloud. **Multi-cloud** uses multiple providers. Both are about flexibility, compliance, and avoiding lock-in.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Azure Arc** projects on-prem/edge resources into Azure management. **AWS Outposts** puts AWS hardware in your DC. **Google Distributed Cloud** extends GCP to edge. Kubernetes is the common control plane across all environments.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: ~75% of enterprises now run multi-cloud. The challenge isn't technology — it's operational consistency: unified IAM, networking, observability, and IaC across environments. Use a cloud-agnostic layer (K8s + Terraform) where portability matters, and go provider-native where differentiation matters.

t-migration.label: Cloud Migration Strategies
t-migration.desc: Moving workloads to the cloud — the 7 Rs, assessment, tooling, and phased execution.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud migration moves workloads from on-premises (or another cloud) to a target cloud. The **7 Rs** define how: rehost, replatform, refactor, repurchase, relocate, retain, or retire.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Rehost** (lift-and-shift) is fastest; **refactor** (re-architect) captures the most cloud value. Start with **discovery and assessment** — inventory apps, map dependencies, classify by migration strategy.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Migrate in waves, not big-bang. Use provider tools (AWS MGN, Azure Migrate, Google Migration Center) for rehosting. Prioritise apps with clear ROI. Expect 30–40% of apps to need refactoring post-migration to realise cost and performance benefits.

t-platform-eng.label: Platform Engineering
t-platform-eng.desc: Building internal developer platforms — golden paths, self-service infrastructure, and developer experience.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Platform engineering** builds a self-service layer on top of cloud infra — so product teams ship without filing tickets or learning Terraform.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: An **Internal Developer Platform (IDP)** provides golden paths: pre-approved templates for deploying services, databases, and pipelines. Tools: **Backstage** (developer portal), **Crossplane** (infrastructure abstraction), **Kratix** (platform APIs).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026 platform engineering is how organisations scale DevOps beyond a few expert teams. The platform team is a product team — its customers are developers. Measure developer experience (time to first deploy, cognitive load) not just infra metrics.

t-sre.label: Site Reliability Engineering
t-sre.desc: SRE principles — SLIs, SLOs, error budgets, incident management, and eliminating toil.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **SRE** applies software engineering to operations. Core idea: define **SLOs** (reliability targets), and use **error budgets** to balance feature velocity against stability.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **SLIs** (service level indicators) measure user-facing quality (latency, availability, correctness). **SLOs** set targets. When error budget is spent, slow down releases and invest in reliability. **Toil** is repetitive manual work that should be automated.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: SRE isn't just on-call. It's a framework: blameless postmortems, runbooks, capacity planning, and progressive rollouts. Google's books remain the gold standard. In 2026, AIOps and LLM-assisted incident triage are augmenting (not replacing) SRE teams.

t-multi-account.label: Multi-Account & Org Strategy
t-multi-account.desc: Structuring cloud accounts for isolation, security, and governance at enterprise scale.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Don't put everything in one account. Use **multiple accounts** (AWS) or subscriptions (Azure) / projects (GCP) to isolate workloads, limit blast radius, and separate billing.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Landing zones** are pre-configured account structures with networking, IAM, logging, and guardrails baked in. AWS Control Tower, Azure Landing Zones, and GCP Fabric all automate this setup.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Typical pattern: separate accounts for security/audit, networking (shared VPCs), workloads (per-team or per-app), and sandboxes. Apply **SCPs** (AWS) or **Org Policies** (GCP) at the OU level to enforce guardrails preventively — no one can accidentally make an S3 bucket public.

t-secrets.label: Secrets Management
t-secrets.desc: Storing, rotating, and accessing sensitive credentials securely — vaults, KMS, and injection patterns.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Never hardcode secrets. Store API keys, DB passwords, and certificates in a **secrets manager** — accessed at runtime, audited, and rotatable.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Cloud-native options: **AWS Secrets Manager**, **Azure Key Vault**, **GCP Secret Manager**. Cross-cloud: **HashiCorp Vault**. Use **automatic rotation** so compromised credentials expire. **KMS** manages encryption keys separately from secrets.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Inject secrets via environment variables, mounted volumes (K8s CSI driver), or SDK calls — never bake into images. Integrate with CI/CD (OIDC federation for short-lived tokens). Audit access logs. In 2026, zero-standing-secrets (dynamic, JIT-generated credentials) is the gold standard.

t-compliance-auto.label: Compliance Automation
t-compliance-auto.desc: Policy-as-code, automated audit evidence, supply chain security, and continuous compliance.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Compliance automation** replaces manual checklists with code that continuously verifies policy — if it's not automated, it drifts.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Policy-as-code** (OPA/Rego, Kyverno, Sentinel) enforces rules on IaC, K8s manifests, and runtime config. **SBOM** (Software Bill of Materials) and **SLSA** levels track supply chain integrity. **Sigstore** provides keyless code signing.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Shift compliance left: validate policies in CI (OPA on Terraform plans, Kyverno on K8s manifests). Generate audit evidence automatically from CloudTrail/Config/Audit Logs. In 2026, frameworks like SOC 2 and ISO 27001 are increasingly satisfied through continuous, automated controls rather than annual audits.

t-cloud-ai.label: Cloud AI/ML Services
t-cloud-ai.desc: Managed AI/ML platforms — model training, serving, GPUs, and MLOps on cloud infrastructure.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud providers offer managed ML platforms that handle the infrastructure for training and serving models — GPUs, distributed training, model registries, and endpoints.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **SageMaker** (AWS), **Vertex AI** (GCP), and **Azure AI Studio** provide end-to-end pipelines: data labelling → training → tuning → deployment → monitoring. GPU instances (P5, A100, H100, TPUs) provide the compute. **MLOps** automates the lifecycle.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: AI/ML is the fastest-growing cloud segment in 2026. Azure leads for OpenAI/GPT integration, GCP for TPUs and BigQuery ML, AWS for the broadest GPU selection. For LLM inference, use managed endpoints (Bedrock, Vertex AI) rather than self-hosting unless you need full control. Track experiments with MLflow, deploy with feature stores and model monitoring.

t-data-pipelines.label: Data Pipelines & Analytics
t-data-pipelines.desc: ETL/ELT, data warehouses, lakehouses, and real-time analytics on cloud data platforms.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Data pipelines move data from sources (databases, APIs, events) into **warehouses** or **lakes** where it's cleaned, transformed, and made queryable for analytics and ML.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **ETL** transforms before loading; **ELT** loads raw then transforms in-warehouse (the modern approach). Warehouses: **BigQuery**, **Redshift**, **Synapse**. **dbt** is the standard transformation layer. The **lakehouse** (Delta Lake, Iceberg) merges lake flexibility with warehouse performance.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, serverless warehouses (BigQuery, Redshift Serverless) dominate — no cluster management, pay per query. Open table formats (**Apache Iceberg**, Delta Lake) prevent vendor lock-in on data lakes. Orchestrate with Airflow, Dagster, or Prefect. Real-time: Kafka → Flink → warehouse for streaming analytics.

t-edge.label: Edge Computing & IoT
t-edge.desc: Running compute closer to users and devices — CDN functions, edge locations, and IoT platforms.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Edge computing** runs code closer to users (CDN edge) or devices (IoT gateways) — reducing latency and bandwidth to the cloud.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Edge functions** (Lambda@Edge, CloudFront Functions, Cloudflare Workers) run at CDN PoPs for ultra-low-latency transformations. **IoT platforms** (IoT Core, IoT Hub) manage device connectivity, telemetry ingestion, and device shadows/twins at scale.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Edge is growing fast — personalization, A/B testing, and auth at the CDN layer. For IoT, manage millions of devices with MQTT and device management services. **Edge AI** (inference on-device or at the edge) avoids round-trips to the cloud for real-time decisions. Google Distributed Cloud and AWS Outposts bring full cloud services to edge locations.

l-on-demand.label: On-Demand Resources
l-on-demand.desc: Provision compute, storage, and databases in seconds via API — no hardware procurement.

l-elasticity.label: Elasticity & Auto-Scaling
l-elasticity.desc: Automatically scale resources up during load spikes and back down when demand drops.

l-shared-responsibility.label: Shared Responsibility Model
l-shared-responsibility.desc: Provider secures the cloud (hardware, network); you secure what's in the cloud (data, config, access).

l-iaas.label: IaaS
l-iaas.desc: Infrastructure-as-a-Service — VMs, networks, and storage. You manage OS, runtime, and app.

l-paas.label: PaaS
l-paas.desc: Platform-as-a-Service — deploy code to a managed runtime. Provider handles OS, scaling, patching.

l-saas.label: SaaS
l-saas.desc: Software-as-a-Service — fully managed apps you use over the internet (Gmail, Salesforce, Slack).

l-faas.label: FaaS / CaaS
l-faas.desc: Functions-as-a-Service and Containers-as-a-Service — hybrid abstractions between PaaS and IaaS.

l-aws.label: AWS
l-aws.desc: Amazon Web Services — largest market share, broadest service catalog (200+ services), most mature ecosystem.

l-azure.label: Azure
l-azure.desc: Microsoft Azure — deepest enterprise/Microsoft integration, hybrid cloud (Arc), strong government compliance.

l-gcp.label: GCP
l-gcp.desc: Google Cloud Platform — leads in data analytics (BigQuery), AI/ML (TPUs, Vertex AI), and Kubernetes (GKE).

l-instance-types.label: Instance Types & Families
l-instance-types.desc: General purpose, compute-optimised, memory-optimised, GPU — pick the right shape for the workload.

l-autoscaling.label: Auto Scaling Groups
l-autoscaling.desc: Automatically adjust VM count based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics to match demand.

l-spot-instances.label: Spot / Preemptible Instances
l-spot-instances.desc: Spare capacity at 60–90% discount — can be interrupted, ideal for fault-tolerant batch workloads.

l-docker.label: Docker
l-docker.desc: The tool that popularised containers — build images with a Dockerfile, run them anywhere.

l-images-registries.label: Images & Registries
l-images-registries.desc: OCI images stored in registries (ECR, ACR, Artifact Registry, Docker Hub) — versioned, scannable, deployable.

l-container-runtime.label: Container Runtimes
l-container-runtime.desc: containerd, CRI-O — the low-level engines that actually run containers on a host.

l-k8s-arch.label: K8s Architecture
l-k8s-arch.desc: Control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager) + worker nodes (kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime).

l-pods-services.label: Pods, Services & Ingress
l-pods-services.desc: Pods group containers; Services provide stable endpoints; Ingress exposes HTTP routes externally.

l-helm.label: Helm Charts
l-helm.desc: Package manager for Kubernetes — template and version complex deployments as reusable charts.

l-service-mesh.label: Service Mesh
l-service-mesh.desc: Istio, Linkerd — add mTLS, traffic shaping, retries, and observability as infrastructure, not app code.

l-lambda.label: Lambda / Cloud Functions
l-lambda.desc: Write a function, upload it, and the provider runs it on any trigger — HTTP, queue, schedule, or event.

l-event-triggers.label: Event Triggers
l-event-triggers.desc: S3 uploads, DynamoDB streams, Pub/Sub messages, HTTP requests — events that invoke your function.

l-cold-starts.label: Cold Starts
l-cold-starts.desc: First-invocation latency when the runtime initialises — mitigate with provisioned concurrency or lightweight runtimes.

l-vpc.label: VPC / Virtual Network
l-vpc.desc: Your isolated cloud network — define IP ranges, route tables, and gateways.

l-subnets.label: Subnets & Route Tables
l-subnets.desc: Public subnets (internet-facing), private subnets (internal), route tables direct traffic between them.

l-load-balancers.label: Load Balancers
l-load-balancers.desc: ALB (Layer 7), NLB (Layer 4) — distribute traffic across healthy targets for availability and scale.

l-dns-cdn.label: DNS & CDN
l-dns-cdn.desc: Route 53 / Cloud DNS for name resolution; CloudFront / Cloud CDN for edge caching and low latency.

l-policies-roles.label: Policies & Roles
l-policies-roles.desc: JSON policy documents define permissions; roles are assumed by services or users without long-lived keys.

l-federation.label: Identity Federation
l-federation.desc: SAML, OIDC — connect your corporate IdP to cloud so users don't need separate cloud credentials.

l-least-privilege.label: Least Privilege
l-least-privilege.desc: Grant only the minimum permissions needed. Use access analysers to find and remove excess.

l-zt-principles.label: Zero Trust Principles
l-zt-principles.desc: Verify explicitly, use least-privilege access, assume breach — applied to every request regardless of network.

l-microsegmentation.label: Microsegmentation
l-microsegmentation.desc: Fine-grained network segmentation between workloads — limits lateral movement if one service is compromised.

l-encryption.label: Encryption (at rest & in transit)
l-encryption.desc: KMS-managed keys encrypt storage; TLS encrypts network traffic. Default to encryption everywhere.

l-compliance.label: Compliance & Governance
l-compliance.desc: SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR — frameworks providers comply with; you must configure your side correctly.

l-cspm.label: CSPM Tools
l-cspm.desc: Cloud Security Posture Management — continuous scanning for misconfigurations against CIS benchmarks.

l-object-storage.label: Object Storage
l-object-storage.desc: S3, Azure Blob, GCS — store unlimited unstructured data with 11 nines durability and lifecycle policies.

l-block-storage.label: Block Storage
l-block-storage.desc: EBS, Managed Disks, Persistent Disks — high-performance volumes attached to VMs for databases and apps.

l-file-storage.label: File Storage
l-file-storage.desc: EFS, Azure Files, Filestore — shared NFS/SMB file systems accessible by multiple instances concurrently.

l-rds-sql.label: Relational (SQL)
l-rds-sql.desc: Managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server — automated backups, replicas, failover. Aurora and AlloyDB for cloud-native performance.

l-nosql.label: NoSQL
l-nosql.desc: DynamoDB, Cosmos DB, Firestore — key-value, document, and wide-column stores for flexible schemas at massive scale.

l-purpose-built.label: Purpose-Built DBs
l-purpose-built.desc: Graph (Neptune), time-series (Timestream), in-memory (ElastiCache/Redis), ledger (QLDB) — choose by access pattern.

l-queues.label: Message Queues
l-queues.desc: SQS, Azure Queue — point-to-point, at-least-once delivery, decouples producer from consumer.

l-pubsub.label: Pub/Sub
l-pubsub.desc: SNS, Google Pub/Sub — fan-out events to multiple subscribers via topics.

l-event-streaming.label: Event Streaming
l-event-streaming.desc: Kafka, Kinesis, EventBridge — ordered, durable, replayable event logs for real-time analytics and event sourcing.

l-terraform.label: Terraform
l-terraform.desc: Declarative IaC in HCL — plan/apply workflow, 4800+ providers, the most widely adopted tool (26M+ weekly downloads).

l-pulumi.label: Pulumi
l-pulumi.desc: IaC in real languages (TypeScript, Python, Go, C#) — full IDE support, testing, and abstractions.

l-opentofu.label: OpenTofu
l-opentofu.desc: Open-source Terraform fork (Linux Foundation) — drop-in compatible, community-driven, no license concerns.

l-cdk.label: CDK / CDKTF
l-cdk.desc: AWS CDK and CDKTF generate CloudFormation/Terraform from imperative code — high-level constructs for common patterns.

l-ci-build-test.label: CI: Build & Test
l-ci-build-test.desc: Every commit triggers: lint, compile, unit tests, integration tests, security scans — fast feedback on breakage.

l-cd-deploy.label: CD: Deploy
l-cd-deploy.desc: Automatically deploy passing builds through environments (dev → staging → prod) with promotion gates.

l-gitops.label: GitOps
l-gitops.desc: Git as the single source of truth — Argo CD / Flux sync cluster state to match what's in the repo.

l-cost-visibility.label: Cost Visibility & Tagging
l-cost-visibility.desc: Tag every resource (team, env, project) so costs are attributable. Dashboards and budgets surface trends.

l-rightsizing.label: Right-Sizing
l-rightsizing.desc: Match instance size to actual utilisation — most VMs use <40% of their allocated CPU.

l-reservations.label: Savings Plans & Reserved Instances
l-reservations.desc: Commit to steady-state usage for 1–3 years and save 30–72% vs. on-demand pricing.

l-metrics.label: Metrics
l-metrics.desc: Numerical time-series (CPU, latency, error rate) — cheap to store, fast to query, ideal for dashboards and alerts.

l-logging.label: Structured Logging
l-logging.desc: JSON logs with correlation IDs — searchable, filterable, shipped to centralized stores (CloudWatch Logs, Loki).

l-tracing.label: Distributed Tracing
l-tracing.desc: Follow a request across services via trace/span IDs — pinpoint where latency or errors are introduced.

l-alerting.label: Alerting & SLOs
l-alerting.desc: Define SLIs/SLOs, alert on error budget burn — page humans only when user experience is at risk.

l-service-decomposition.label: Service Decomposition
l-service-decomposition.desc: Split by business domain (DDD bounded contexts), not by tech layer — each service owns its data and deploys independently.

l-api-gateway.label: API Gateway
l-api-gateway.desc: A single entry point that handles routing, auth, rate limiting, and request transformation for backend services.

l-api-versioning.label: API Versioning & Contracts
l-api-versioning.desc: Version APIs (URL path, header) and use contract testing (Pact) to prevent breaking changes between services.

l-ddd.label: Domain-Driven Design
l-ddd.desc: Bounded contexts, aggregates, and ubiquitous language — the strategic patterns that guide service boundaries.

l-rpo-rto.label: RPO & RTO
l-rpo-rto.desc: Recovery Point Objective (max data loss) and Recovery Time Objective (max downtime) — define your tolerance.

l-multi-az.label: Multi-AZ Deployments
l-multi-az.desc: Spread across availability zones for HA — survives single data-centre failures with no manual intervention.

l-multi-region.label: Multi-Region Architecture
l-multi-region.desc: Active-passive or active-active across regions for DR — handles full regional outages at higher cost and complexity.

l-chaos-eng.label: Chaos Engineering
l-chaos-eng.desc: Inject failures (AWS FIS, Gremlin, Litmus) in controlled experiments to prove your system's resilience before real outages.

l-hybrid-patterns.label: Hybrid Patterns
l-hybrid-patterns.desc: Burst-to-cloud, tiered (on-prem data / cloud compute), edge hybrid — pick the pattern that matches your constraint.

l-arc-outposts.label: Arc / Outposts / Distributed Cloud
l-arc-outposts.desc: Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, Google Distributed Cloud — extend cloud management and APIs to on-prem and edge.

l-multi-cloud-strategy.label: Multi-Cloud Strategy
l-multi-cloud-strategy.desc: Use cloud-agnostic layers (K8s, Terraform) where portability matters; go provider-native where differentiation matters.

l-7rs.label: The 7 Rs
l-7rs.desc: Rehost, replatform, refactor, repurchase, relocate, retain, retire — a decision framework for each workload.

l-migration-assessment.label: Discovery & Assessment
l-migration-assessment.desc: Inventory apps, map dependencies, assess cloud-readiness, and classify by migration strategy.

l-migration-tooling.label: Migration Tooling
l-migration-tooling.desc: AWS MGN, Azure Migrate, Google Migration Center — automate server replication, database migration, and cutover.

l-idp.label: Internal Developer Platform
l-idp.desc: A self-service product layer that abstracts infra complexity — devs deploy services without learning cloud internals.

l-golden-paths.label: Golden Paths & Templates
l-golden-paths.desc: Pre-approved, opinionated templates for common workloads — service scaffolds with CI/CD, observability, and security built in.

l-backstage.label: Backstage & Portals
l-backstage.desc: Spotify's Backstage — a developer portal with service catalog, docs, and plugin ecosystem for your IDP.

l-sli-slo.label: SLIs & SLOs
l-sli-slo.desc: Service Level Indicators measure quality (latency p99, error rate); Service Level Objectives set your reliability targets.

l-error-budgets.label: Error Budgets
l-error-budgets.desc: 100% minus your SLO = budget for unreliability. Spend it on feature velocity; when it's exhausted, prioritise stability.

l-incident-mgmt.label: Incident Management
l-incident-mgmt.desc: On-call rotations, severity levels, war rooms, blameless postmortems — a structured response to production issues.

l-toil.label: Toil Elimination
l-toil.desc: Repetitive, manual operational work that scales linearly with service size — automate it or engineer it away.

l-org-structure.label: Org / Account Hierarchy
l-org-structure.desc: Org → OUs → Accounts (AWS) / Management Groups → Subscriptions (Azure) / Org → Folders → Projects (GCP).

l-landing-zones.label: Landing Zones
l-landing-zones.desc: Pre-built, best-practice account structures with networking, logging, and security guardrails out of the box.

l-scps-guardrails.label: SCPs & Preventive Guardrails
l-scps-guardrails.desc: Service Control Policies (AWS), Azure Policy, Org Policies (GCP) — enforce security at the org level before anyone can violate them.

l-secret-stores.label: Secret Stores
l-secret-stores.desc: AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, HashiCorp Vault — centralized, audited, access-controlled.

l-rotation.label: Automatic Rotation
l-rotation.desc: Rotate credentials on a schedule (30/60/90 days) so compromised secrets expire automatically.

l-injection-patterns.label: Secret Injection Patterns
l-injection-patterns.desc: Env vars, K8s CSI driver mounts, SDK calls at runtime — never bake secrets into images or repos.

l-policy-as-code.label: Policy-as-Code
l-policy-as-code.desc: OPA/Rego, Kyverno, Sentinel — write compliance rules as code, enforce in CI and at admission time.

l-supply-chain.label: Supply Chain Security
l-supply-chain.desc: SBOM, SLSA levels, Sigstore signing — verify provenance and integrity of every artifact in your pipeline.

l-audit-evidence.label: Automated Audit Evidence
l-audit-evidence.desc: Generate SOC 2 / ISO 27001 evidence from CloudTrail, Config rules, and policy engine results — continuously, not annually.

l-ml-platforms.label: Managed ML Platforms
l-ml-platforms.desc: SageMaker, Vertex AI, Azure AI Studio — end-to-end pipelines from data prep to model serving.

l-gpu-instances.label: GPU & Accelerator Instances
l-gpu-instances.desc: P5, H100, A100, TPU v5 — specialised instances for training and inference at scale.

l-mlops.label: MLOps
l-mlops.desc: ML lifecycle automation: experiment tracking (MLflow), model registry, automated retraining, drift detection, and monitoring.

l-etl-elt.label: ETL / ELT
l-etl-elt.desc: Extract-Transform-Load (classic) vs. Extract-Load-Transform (modern, in-warehouse with dbt).

l-data-warehouse.label: Data Warehouses
l-data-warehouse.desc: BigQuery, Redshift, Synapse — columnar, MPP engines optimised for analytical queries over large datasets.

l-lakehouse.label: Lakehouse & Open Formats
l-lakehouse.desc: Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake — open table formats that give data lakes warehouse features (ACID, time-travel, schema evolution).

l-edge-functions.label: Edge Functions
l-edge-functions.desc: Lambda@Edge, CloudFront Functions, Cloudflare Workers — run code at CDN PoPs for sub-10ms response.

l-iot-platforms.label: IoT Platforms
l-iot-platforms.desc: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google IoT — device provisioning, MQTT messaging, digital twins, and fleet management.

l-edge-ai.label: Edge AI / On-Device Inference
l-edge-ai.desc: Run ML models at the edge (Greengrass, Azure IoT Edge) to avoid cloud round-trips for real-time decisions.
